questions.question: Sher Shah took many steps for the welfare of people. Give some examples to justify this statement.
questions.answer: Sher Shah was an able ruler who genuinely cared about the welfare of his subjects. He constructed important roads including the Grand Trunk Road from Sonargoan in Bengal to Peshawar, with trees planted on both sides and sarais (rest houses) for travellers. His land revenue system was fair, with the state's share being one-third of produce, recorded in a Patta to prevent overcharging. During famines, advances were made to peasants. He abolished most trade duties to promote commerce, introduced uniform weights and measures, and standardized coins. His provincial administration ensured justice through regular transfer of officers. He showed religious tolerance and his overall reforms benefited common people greatly.
key_points: Grand Trunk Road with trees, wells, and sarais for travellers
Fair land revenue system with Patta to prevent overcharging
Relief during famines and calamities, advances to peasants
Abolished most trade duties to promote commerce
Uniform weights, measures, and standardized coins
Religious tolerance and welfare measures for common people
Transfer of officers every 2-3 years to prevent corruption

questions.question: Why do you think Babur's decision to stay in India after the Battle of Panipat was a turning point in Indian history?
questions.answer: Babur's decision to stay in India after Panipat was a turning point because it led to the establishment of the Mughal Empire, which would go on to rule India for over 300 years. If Babur had returned to Central Asia as his nobles wished, and as Daulat Khan Lodi and Rana Sanga expected, the political landscape of India would have been entirely different. By staying, Babur consolidated his position through victories at Khanua, Chanderi, and Ghaghara, weakening both Rajput and Afghan resistance. This created the foundation for one of the largest and most influential empires in Indian history, which contributed significantly to Indian art, architecture, administration, and culture.
key_points: Led to establishment of the Mughal Empire lasting over 300 years
Nobles wanted to return to Central Asia but Babur convinced them to stay
Daulat Khan Lodi and Rana Sanga expected Babur to leave after Panipat
Subsequent victories at Khanua, Chanderi, and Ghaghara consolidated Mughal power
Weakened both Rajput and Afghan resistance
Foundation for one of India's most influential empires

questions.question: Compare the military skills of Babur and Humayun. Why do you think Humayun failed where Babur succeeded?
questions.answer: Babur was an able military commander who won decisive battles through superior tactics, the Rumi method of warfare, and innovative use of artillery. He commanded the respect of his nobility and could convince reluctant nobles to stay in India. Humayun, on the other hand, was described as kind and generous but not a good general or warrior. He did not command the respect of the Mughal nobility, and the Chaghatai nobles were not favourably inclined towards him. Humayun also faced the additional problem of disloyal brothers who wanted his throne. While Babur dealt with external threats through military genius, Humayun was overwhelmed by both internal family conflicts and external enemies like Sher Shah, who was himself a brilliant military strategist.
key_points: Babur was an able military commander; Humayun was not a good general
Babur commanded respect of nobility; Humayun did not
Babur used innovative Rumi method and artillery; Humayun lacked tactical innovation
Humayun faced disloyal brothers competing for the throne
Sher Shah was a brilliant strategist who outmatched Humayun
Babur could inspire and convince his reluctant followers

questions.question: Do you think Sher Shah deserves to be called one of the greatest administrators of medieval India? Justify your answer.
questions.answer: Yes, Sher Shah deserves to be called one of the greatest administrators of medieval India. Despite ruling for only about five years (1540-1545), his reforms had a lasting impact. His provincial administration with 47 sarkars, regular transfer of officers, and village-level governance through Panchayats was well-organized. His land revenue system was so efficient that even the great Mughal emperor Akbar later adopted it. His military reforms including the Dagh system and personal recruitment ensured a disciplined army. The Grand Trunk Road and network of sarais transformed trade and communication. His standardized currency (silver rupia, gold mohurs, copper dams) and uniform weights and measures facilitated commerce. These achievements in such a short reign demonstrate extraordinary administrative ability.
key_points: Ruled only about five years but left lasting reforms
Land revenue system adopted by Akbar himself
47 sarkars with efficient provincial administration
Grand Trunk Road transformed trade and communication
Standardized currency used for centuries
Military reforms including Dagh system
Short reign makes his achievements even more remarkable

questions.question: How did the use of artillery and cannons by Babur change the nature of warfare in India?
questions.answer: The use of artillery and cannons by Babur fundamentally changed the nature of warfare in India. Before Babur, Indian armies relied primarily on cavalry, war elephants, and infantry with traditional weapons. Babur's use of the Rumi (Ottoman) method of warfare with gunpowder weapons gave him a decisive advantage at Panipat despite having fewer soldiers. The loud noise of cannons terrified the enemy's horses and elephants, creating chaos in their ranks. This demonstrated that a smaller but technologically superior force could defeat a much larger traditional army. After Babur's introduction of artillery, subsequent rulers in India also had to adopt gunpowder weapons, making them a standard part of military forces. This shift from reliance on numbers to reliance on technology and tactics marked a new era in Indian military history.
key_points: Before Babur, Indian armies relied on cavalry, elephants, and traditional weapons
Rumi (Ottoman) method introduced gunpowder warfare to India
Smaller force could defeat larger army with superior technology
Cannon noise terrified enemy horses and elephants
Subsequent rulers had to adopt gunpowder weapons
Marked shift from reliance on numbers to technology and tactics
